title: Australia and EU Seal Historic $7B Trade and Security Pact After Eight Years
description: Australia and the European Union have signed a massive $7 billion free trade and security agreement, eliminating most tariffs and boosting defense cooperation.
lede: After nearly a decade of negotiations, Australia and the European Union have officially signed a sweeping $7 billion trade and security agreement. The deal eliminates almost all tariffs and establishes a new strategic partnership focused on defense and critical minerals amid shifting global power dynamics.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Australia and the European Union signed a landmark free trade agreement on Tuesday, officially concluding eight years of intense negotiations. Valued at approximately A$10 billion ($7 billion; £5.2 billion), the deal removes almost all tariffs on trade between the two economic powers.

Under the terms of the agreement, almost all EU tariffs will be lifted on Australian agricultural exports, including wine, fruit, vegetables, olive oil, seafood, dairy, wheat, and barley. The Australian government estimates this will save local wine producers and exporters roughly A$37 million.

In return, Australian consumers will see cheaper prices on European goods like wine, spirits, biscuits, chocolates, and pasta. The deal also resolves long-standing disputes over naming rights: Australian producers can continue to sell Italian-style sparkling wine domestically as "prosecco," though the name will be phased out for exports over 10 years.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The agreement represents a major strategic alignment for both regions. European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen highlighted the deal's focus on "collective resilience" in a deeply changing world. She specifically pointed to the vulnerability of global supply chains and the weaponization of tariffs by great powers.

Beyond agriculture and consumer goods, the pact establishes a new security and defense partnership. The two powers agreed to increase cooperation in the defense industry, counter-terrorism, space exploration, and maritime security, signaling a bond that extends far beyond pure economics.

Not everyone is celebrating the terms, however. Meat and Livestock Australia criticized the deal on meat exports, calling it a "missed opportunity." While the agreement increases the annual quota for Australian meat from 3,389 tonnes to about 30,000 tonnes, it falls significantly short of the 50,000 tonnes local farmers had demanded.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The trade pact serves as a strategic counterweight to the economic influence of other global superpowers. Von der Leyen's comments regarding powers using "tariffs as leverage" are widely interpreted as a reference to US trade policies under Donald Trump, as well as China's dominance in the critical minerals sector.

To address these supply chain vulnerabilities, the EU and Australia also announced greater cooperation on several critical minerals projects. This includes strategic resources like lithium and tungsten, which are essential for modern technology and the transition to green energy.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: With the ink now dry on the agreement, both regions will begin the process of implementing the tariff reductions and integrating their defense and mineral supply chains. The phased transitions, such as the 10-year sunset on the export use of the name "prosecco" and the lengthy phase-out periods for names like feta, will dictate the pace of integration for specific industries.

The success of the critical minerals partnership will be closely watched by the international community as western allies increasingly seek to secure independent supply chains away from Chinese dominance.
